text: Skip has a few unusual aspects. For one, the editor lets you see not only the parse tree, but the AST. Not many languages let you see this. It also is evidently a transpiler to JS as well as a compiled language. That must have been a lot of work to reconcile those two universes (presuming they use LLVM or something to emit machine code). This was obviously a huge project, representing millions in R&D dollars. From first glance they embraced OOP as a paradigm, and that might have been one of their downfalls.

Skip has a few unusual aspects. For one, the editor lets you see not only the parse tree, but the AST. Not many languages let you see this. It also is evidently a transpiler to JS as well as a compiled language. That must have been a lot of work to reconcile those two universes (presuming they use LLVM or something to emit machine code). This was obviously a huge project, representing millions in R&D dollars. From first glance they embraced OOP as a paradigm, and that might have been one of their downfalls.

But commercial success or failure does not always mean the product was good or bad. There are many bad things that became popular, like COBOL, and many good things like the Pierce-Arrow car or Modula-2 that never caught on. So i am not casting shade on the Skip project which i spent 10 minutes studying.

text: one of the things i've always found unsettling about ethereum and urbit is that they both embed these really powerful capitalist notions of distinct ownership into their models
one of the things i've always found unsettling about ethereum and urbit is that they both embed these really powerful capitalist notions of distinct ownership into their models
in addition to the well-documented problems of pseudo-currency speculation it's just not clear to me that this is an obviously central feature of a distributed computing environment
in addition to the well-documented problems of pseudo-currency speculation it's just not clear to me that this is an obviously central feature of a distributed computing environment

there are certainly use-cases for incentivizing someone else to run your code while your computer is offline -- perhaps trading in a commodities market or watching for particular news stories -- but it seems more common that we want to run our own software locally on our own computers. i want to organize a soccer game, balance my chequebook, calculate the risk of a catastrophic asteroid impact in the next 20 years. it's not clear to me that any of these systems are well-suited to that kind of work.

There aren't a lot of great books on programming, but i can recommend Programs = Algorithms + Data Structures by Prof. Wirth of ETH. He invented Pascal, Modula-2, Oberon, the Lilith computer. His Modula-2 language was the high-water mark in the 70's for languages, and aspects of it have not been matched yet in any other language i am aware of. I would burn "Code Complete". Any company that has over a million outstanding unresolved bugs in their OS has no business writing a book on how to program properly. By any objective measurement, MS is one of the most inefficient companies in the history of computers. The previous most inefficient company was IBM, and indeed MS is better than they were, but seriously their systems are like swiss cheese.  Wirth's books are short, economical, beautiful work.

<@UFXFB1RK8> I think the use of cryptocurrency to fuel computation (ETH) or storage (FileCoin) is an interesting experiment in answering the question of how to support computation processes or preservation of data that is valued by a society, without some of the pathologies and failure modes of the current models.

Right now many large-scale computations & data aggregations are kept alive by VC-funded corporations (which often leads to a personal data exploiting model) or motivated individuals (which depends on the continued good health and motivation of the individual).

Attaching a currency directly to these processes is one way that society could express “we want this computation/data to be preserved”, in such a way that when that interest dwindles, the shared resource is effectively garbage-collected.

(There’s also the related topic of how society expresses support for particular code to be maintained and evolved, on which Nadia Eghbal has done excellent work.)

<@UFXFB1RK8> I think the use of cryptocurrency to fuel computation (ETH) or storage (FileCoin) is an interesting experiment in answering the question of how to support computation processes or preservation of data that is valued by a society, without some of the pathologies and failure modes of the current models.

Right now many large-scale computations & data aggregations are kept alive by VC-funded corporations (which often leads to a personal data exploiting model) or motivated individuals (which depends on the continued good health and motivation of the individual).

Attaching a currency directly to these processes is one way that society could express “we want this computation/data to be preserved”, in such a way that when that interest dwindles, the shared resource is effectively garbage-collected.

(There’s also the related topic of how society expresses support for particular code to be maintained and evolved, on which Nadia Eghbal has done excellent work.)

I do believe that there are many valuable data aggregations and computations that make more sense to be shared by many users than (expensively) re-derived by every individual’s computing node: for instance, web search indices, weather predictions, public transport routing graphs and live data caches (my own current field)

I don't think all cryptocurrencies are necessarily unethical, but I don't think we should redesign the fundament of computing to encode ownership and introduce scarcity. We swim in an ocean of underutilized computers. Perhaps there's an option to better enable the free collaboration humans are already so good at? Think of SETI at Home, the protein folding project, heck, even the vast community of media pirates sharing their favorite content with one another.

I’m with you in terms of wanting to look for collaborative models. That said, CPU cycles (or rather the power to run them), storage space, and human attention are all finite. We run out of battery & flash space on our personal computers, and certainly it incurs some mental overhead to establish that we’re only donating our CPU cycles to folding@home and not a rogue bitcoin miner.

If computation and storage are unlimited, why are there so many broken web links?

As Ted Nelson pointed out in his video, he was a proponent of two-way links, and this would have solved a lot of problems, however, the exponential growth of the internet was partially fueled by intentional violations of copyright. Ted comes from a writer/publisher's point of view, and from that point of view the current web architecture is a disaster. The proliferation of paywalls, and subscriptions is lamentable, and his proposal for micropayments was very sound, and if cryptocurrencies had existed at his time, it might have won the day.  Anyway back to Urbit, it is a very creative concept, but there are many things best forgotten, and i am not sure it is feasible to keep track of all changes of all things for all time. That sounds like a lot of wasted storage. I think intuitively people realize that only some transactions are worth recording for posterity.

The Requiem by Maurice Durflé is a killer work. He takes you straight to heaven. If you are interested in mystic music, you might look at Scriabin's piano works. He was working on a super-work called the Mysterium that would cause spontaneous enlightenment. He was a very interesting fellow. Also post-awakening John Coltrane is wonderful stuff. Myself for programming i used Anoushka Shankar to power the first year of my project. She's better than her father was, and he was great. Rhythmic music helps with typing.

The aspect I find most appealing is having 'one portal' for all stuff. Currently I log into portal#1 (my laptop) - here I get my local files and apps only. One app (firefox) has me log in to portals 2, 3, 4 (gmail/gdrive, github, <http://futureofocoding.slack.com|futureofocoding.slack.com>) - each one completely siloed away - and has its own set of artifacts. I have various systems in place to sync and various rudimentary import/export mechanisms to move stuff around between these portals, and the nature of computing/programming is very different too.
On the web, every app is in fact a new portal to a new siloed world. I hope we have have one world where all artifacts are possible, can interlink etc and we can program here too.

thanks - looks bonkers! I'm not sure if I'd find it fun or a punishment :smile:

having one infrastructure with access to everything sounds impossible to me. It sounds impossible because it would require all programmers to agree on what is the correct way to do things and to never add anything new without years of committees of getting everyone's agreements that this new thing is being done the "right" way.

Portals (as defined above) seem as natural as the fact that I have different thoughts than the person sitting next to me. Those thoughts lead to different solutions and different solutions => silos.

Interesting counterpoint wrt portals. Perhaps we can't get rid of portals completely. But still, silos by design is one thing and often we have silos by accident - it's easier to create silos, even when I want to create open, extensible apps and features. The baseline of interoperability today is importing/exporting 'files' and rudimentary copy/paste. Can this be raised?

text: How on earth did I forget to include Scriabin among my list of fascinating composers?! (Latter Scriabin of course, e.g. the two Symponies/Poems and as you mentioned, the piano works). Mysterium - wasn’t that a work to be performed on a mountain in India during the end of the world? Got to get into this stuff more! Too much careerism can sap one’s spirituality. :slightly_smiling_face:

Never quite got into John Coltrane, but Sun Ra and Parliament Funkadelic interested me a lot with themes of space and transcendence (I experience “Maggot Brain” as simply pure meditation). Will check out Maurice Durfle.

Coltrane ranges from super mellow to unbearable atonality/harshness. The album he did with Johnny Hartman is one of the most mellow records you will ever hear. There is also a compilation called the "gentle side of JC" that is particularly pleasant. His wife Alice Coltrane was no slouch either, and she made a lot of spacey harp music at the same time as he was doing his stuff. Coltrane got into heroin and then kicked it so there an intensity in his work, depending on his physical state, that is quite extreme. And of course you are getting McCoy Tyner who is super great. Tyner still performs a bit, he is one of the only survivors of that era. sorry i misspelled Durufle earlier. He wrote very little music, but his Requiem i believe is even nicer than Faure's. And also worth checking out is Cannonball Adderly & Nancy Wilson. She was just a kid at the time, her first record, and it is spectacular. Youth is so beautiful.
